topical_tags.term: Miktzas Hayom Kekulo (Part of a day counts as a whole day)
Rav Masna (Position: 30 days in principle, Miktzas Hayom applies)
Bar Pada (Position: 29 full days in principle, no Miktzas Hayom)
Rabbi Eliezer (Position: Only 7 days for Tumah; implies 29 days as standard)
Chachamim (Position: 30 days required; Tumah on day 30 invalidates entire Nezirut)
Oleh Lakan Ulakan (A day counts toward both the ending and beginning periods)
Soter Hakol (Tumah invalidates/undermines the entire Nezirut)
Tumah/Tamei (Becoming impure; invalidates or complicates Nezirut)
Korban/Korbanos (Offering brought to complete Nazirut)
Lechatchila (The preferred, preliminary halachic standard)
Bedieved (Post-facto; after the fact acceptance)
Reish Lakish (Explains Bar Pada's position using special Halacha)
Rubo Kekulo (The majority counts as the whole)
Melot Hayamim / Ad Melot Hayamim (Until the fullness/completion of days)
Rabbi Yoshiya (Opinion: Hair growth requires 30 days minimum)
Rabbi Yonatan (Opinion: Melot Hayamim means inclusive, hence 30 days)
Gematria Yihye (Numerical value of 'yihye' = 30)
Zava / Niddah (Woman with bleeding; requires 7 clean days before immersion)
Mikvah (Ritual bath for purification)
Tevul Yom (One who immersed but awaits nightfall for full purification)
Tahor (Pure state)
Rava (Amora who explains how journey context changes distance to time)
Tosafot (Medieval commentary; provides extensive analysis especially on this daf)
Shechita (Ritual slaughter; requires cutting majority of trachea and esophagus)
Eiruv (Enclosure for Shabbat purposes)
Stam Nezirus (Nazirut without specified duration)
Nazir Olam (Nazirut for life)
Halacha (Jewish law)
Rabbanan of Kisari (Rabbis who taught that years are counted by months, not days)
Parsah / Parsa'ot (Ancient unit of distance, approximately 4 miles)
Melot Hayamim and the measure of duration
